result: I keep thinking about the way light pools on the kitchen counter at 3 p.m., how it looks like someone spilled melted lemon sorbet and forgot to wipe it up. That light makes me want to bite the air, to swallow the whole afternoon in one gulp before it slides off the edge of the day. My grandmother used to say the soul is heaviest at exactly this hour, when the world pauses between lunch and dinner, when clocks forget their purpose and just tick for the music of it. She’d sit at the same counter shelling peas into a chipped enamel bowl, humming something that predated radios, something that tasted like rain on copper roofs. I didn’t know then that she was teaching me to listen to time instead of watching it, to cup my hands around the warm hollow where past and future breathe the same steam. Now I measure years in dish towels: the red-checked one that dried my daughter’s baby bottles, the frayed blue stripe that wiped blood from my first heartbreak, the one with faded lobsters bought on a vacation that felt like cheating on my ordinary life. They hang in consecutive order, a prayer flag of domestic miracles. When the sun hits them just right, they glow like stained glass, and I understand that holiness isn’t a place you visit but lint you carry in your pockets, proof that you kept showing up, kept stirring, kept letting the light in even when it hurt your eyes.
